title: Penalty Won — Obvious Diving Not Caught
summary: A clear dive in the box was awarded as a penalty, directly changing the outcome of the knockout match.
description: During a tense quarter-final with the score tied, an Argentina attacker went down in the penalty area under minimal — if any — contact from the Dutch defender. The referee awarded a penalty despite the attacker having initiated the contact and gone to ground without being legitimately impeded. Replays showed the defender had withdrawn his leg and made no contact sufficient to cause a fall. VAR reviewed the decision but declined to overturn it, despite the incident meeting the threshold of a 'clear and obvious error.' The penalty was converted and Argentina advanced. The decision was widely condemned by pundits and former referees as a clear case of simulation (diving) being rewarded.

images.caption: The angle shows minimal to no contact before the attacker went down.

rules.explanation: Law 12 defines what constitutes a direct free kick offence, including 'trips or attempts to trip an opponent.' However, simulation (diving) is explicitly considered unsporting behaviour and should result in a yellow card for the offending player. Awarding a penalty for a dive is a clear misinterpretation of Law 12 — the player should have been cautioned for simulation rather than being awarded a penalty kick.

Law 5.3 states the referee 'punishes the more serious offence, in terms of sanction, restart, physical severity and tactical impact, when more than one offence occurs at the same time.' Here, the referee punished a non-existent offence while missing the real offence (simulation). VAR should have intervened under the VAR protocol for a 'clear and obvious error' related to a penalty decision.

varOutcome: VAR reviewed but did not overturn — widely considered a clear and obvious error missed.
